title: Comparative & Superlative — Bigger, Biggest
description: Compare things like a native: faster than, bigger than, the best. German builds comparisons with tidy endings — plus a few high-frequency irregulars you'll use every day.

lessons.title: The Comparative — '-er als'
lessons.subtitle: Add -er and use 'als' for 'than'.
lessons.hook: English says 'faster', 'bigger', 'more beautiful.' German keeps it simple: almost every adjective just adds -er, and 'than' is always 'als'. No 'more' needed — even long words: 'interessanter' (more interesting). One ending does the whole job.
lessons.explain: To compare two things, add -er to the adjective and use 'als' for 'than': schnell → schneller, 'Ich bin schneller als du' (I'm faster than you). Short adjectives with a, o, u often add an umlaut too: alt → älter, groß → größer, jung → jünger. A few are irregular and must be memorized: gut → besser (good → better), viel → mehr (much → more), gern → lieber (gladly → rather), hoch → höher. Use 'so … wie' for 'as … as': 'so groß wie' (as big as).

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: He is taller than I am.

vocabulary.memoryHook: IRREGULAR: gut → besser → am besten. Memorize this one cold.
Always 'als' for 'than' in a comparison. (Different from 'wie' = as/like.)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'than' in a comparison?
exercises.explanation: German uses 'als' for 'than': 'schneller als', 'größer als'. Use 'wie' only for 'as…as' (so groß wie).
gut is irregular: gut → besser → am besten (good → better → best). This is one of the most-used adjectives, so memorize it.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Berlin ist ___ als München.' (Berlin is bigger than Munich.) — groß
exercises.explanation: groß → größer (with umlaut). 'Berlin ist größer als München.'

lessons.title: The Superlative — 'am …-sten'
lessons.subtitle: The most, the biggest, the best.
lessons.hook: 'The fastest', 'the biggest', 'the best.' To top a comparison, German uses a neat formula: am + adjective + -sten. 'am schnellsten', 'am größten', 'am besten'. It looks unusual at first, but it's a single reliable pattern.
lessons.explain: The most common superlative form is 'am …-sten' (used after verbs / as an adverb): schnell → am schnellsten, groß → am größten. If the adjective ends in -d, -t, -s, -z, add -esten for pronunciation: alt → am ältesten. Irregulars carry through: gut → am besten, viel → am meisten, gern → am liebsten. When the superlative sits BEFORE a noun, it takes the article + -ste ending: 'der schnellste Zug' (the fastest train), 'die beste Idee' (the best idea).

examples.english: I like drinking coffee most (of all).

vocabulary.memoryHook: am + schnell + sten. The standard superlative shape.
gut → besser → am besten. Fully irregular; extremely common.

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: My favourite thing to eat is pizza.
vocabulary.memoryHook: gern → lieber → am liebsten. Use it to name your favourite.
die Idee → die beste Idee (superlative before a noun).
Irregular: hoch → höher → am höchsten (the 'c' drops in comparative).

exercises.prompt: What is the standard superlative pattern (after a verb)?
exercises.explanation: The adverbial superlative is 'am …-sten': am schnellsten, am größten, am besten.
gut → besser → am besten. This irregular chain is one of the most useful to know by heart.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Anna läuft ___ .' (Anna runs the fastest.) — schnell
exercises.explanation: schnell → am schnellsten. 'Anna läuft am schnellsten.'
